critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of building a simple Unix shell named 'andsh' in C, covering features like command execution, environment variable expansion, piping, and basic interactive enhancements such as history and tab completion. It provides code snippets and explanations of key shell functionalities and system calls used in the implementation.
mainPoints: The author builds a toy shell 'andsh' supporting basic commands, environment variable expansion, and pipes.
The shell uses Unix system calls like fork, execvp, waitpid, and pipe to manage processes and inter-process communication.
Built-in commands like 'cd' are handled within the shell process to affect the shell's state.
The shell implements a read-eval-print loop (REPL) with line editing, history, and tab completion using the readline library.
The shell is intentionally minimal and incomplete, lacking features like quoting, redirection, and advanced builtins.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the process of implementing a basic Unix shell in C, including the use of standard system calls and shell concepts such as process forking, command execution, environment variable expansion, and piping. The code snippets and explanations align with established Unix shell programming practices. The author also correctly notes limitations and missing features, reflecting a realistic scope for a toy shell. The technical details and terminology used are consistent with documented behavior of Unix shells and system calls.
considerations: The article does not cover security implications of shell implementation, such as handling untrusted input or avoiding injection vulnerabilities.
It omits discussion of more advanced shell features like job control, signal handling beyond interrupts, and scripting capabilities.
Performance considerations and resource management for a production-quality shell are not addressed.
The article assumes familiarity with C programming and Unix system calls, which may limit accessibility for beginners.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the challenges and learning experiences involved in building a shell, particularly focusing on string parsing, job control, and process management. Several commenters share personal projects and resources, highlighting both the complexity and educational value of such exercises.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The pipe section is the part that changes how you think about processes. Once you've manually done the dup2 dance — close write-end in parent, close read-end in child, wire them up — it stops being magic and starts being obvious why `grep | sort | uniq` works at all. The thing that surprised me building a similar toy was how late in the process job control has to come: you can get a working pipe chain surprisingly fast, and then job control (SIGTSTP, tcsetpgrp, the whole mess) costs 5x more than everything else combined.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate insight into the complexity of implementing pipes and job control in shells, reflecting a deep understanding of process management.

comments.author: lvales
comments.text:
Building a shell is a great exercise, but honestly having to deal with string parsing is such a bother that it robs like 2/3 of the joy along the way. I once built a very simple one in Go [0] as a learning exercise and I stopped once I started getting frustrated with all the corner cases.

[0] https://github.com/lourencovales/codecrafters/blob/master/sh...
comments.analysis: The comment accurately reflects the common frustration with string parsing in shell building and shares a personal experience, adding credibility.

comments.author: rrampage
comments.text:
Fun read! I built a minimal Linux shell [0] in c and Zig last year which does not depend on libc. It was a great way to learn about execve, the new-ish clone3 syscall and how Linux starts a process. Parsing strings is the least fun part of the building the shell.

[0] https://gist.github.com/rrampage/5046b60ca2d040bcffb49ee38e8...
comments.analysis: This comment adds value by sharing experience with building a shell without libc and confirms the challenge of string parsing, supporting common themes in the discussion.

comments.author: hexer303
comments.text: Unix shells are conceptually simple but hide a surprising amount of complexity under the hood that we take for granted. I recently had build my own PTY controller. There were so many edge-cases to deal with. It took weeks of stress testing and writing many tests to get it right.
comments.analysis: Provides a realistic perspective on the hidden complexity of shell components like PTY controllers, emphasizing the effort required for robustness.

comments.author: emersion
comments.text: Some time ago I've written an article about a particular aspect of shells, job control: https://emersion.fr/blog/2019/job-control/
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant resource on job control, enhancing the discussion with external expert content.

comments.author: ratzkewatzke
comments.text: There's a very good exercise on Codecrafters (https://app.codecrafters.io/courses/shell/overview) to walk you through writing your own shell. I found it enlightening, as well as a good way to learn a new language.
comments.analysis: Recommends a practical learning resource, contributing positively to the conversation.

comments.author: mzs
comments.text:
Had an assignment to build a shell in a week, how hard could it be?

  controlling terminal
  session leader
  job control
The parser was easy in comparison.
comments.analysis: Highlights the relative difficulty of job control compared to parsing, aligning with other comments about complexity.

comments.author: doe88
comments.text: Is there a (real) shell whose code is relatively short and self contained and would be valuable to read? This was always something I wanted to do but never quite spent time to look for a good one to explore.
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about accessible shell codebases, encouraging further discussion and resource sharing.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: Bit of pedantry but I don't think traditional unix shell (like this) follows repl model; the shell is not usually doing printing of the result of evaluation. Instead the printing happens more as a side effect of the commands.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced clarification about shell behavior, showing attention to detail and understanding of shell design.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: Interesting. I wanted to do toast | bash to let the AI drive the computer but the bash shell really got in the way. Too much complexity. The things that annoy humans, $ expansion, special characters, etc don't work for AI either. Ended up writing a custom shell for AI (and humans). When a tool gets in the way, sometimes it just time to change the tool.
comments.analysis: Shares an innovative use case and practical experience, illustrating how shell complexity can motivate custom solutions.

comments.author: lasgawe
comments.text: Great article. There are many things every developer should do when starting to learn programming or when trying to improve their skills. This is one of them. I once built a shell-like programming language (not an interpreter). If anyone reading this wants to improve their skills, I strongly suggest building your own shell from scratch.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and encouragement, promoting shell building as a valuable learning exercise.

comments.author: austy69
comments.text: Fun read. Wonder if you are able to edit text in the shell, or if you need to implement a gap buffer to allow it?
comments.analysis: Raises a technical question about shell text editing, contributing to deeper technical discussion.

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: Link was previously posted by author: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47398749 There are other good quality articles on their site, and maybe deserves the imaginary points.
comments.analysis: Adds little substantive content, mostly pointing out a repost and general praise.
